data.title: Hong Kong issues its first batch of stablecoin licenses: Why these two out of 36?
data.description:
Source: Hong Kong Monetary Authority; Compiled by: Jinse Finance

Summary

On April 10, 2026, the Hong Kong Monetary Authority (HKMA) announced that the Commissioner of Monetary Authority (CMMA) has granted stablecoin issuer licenses to Identifier Financial Technology Co., Ltd. and The Hongkong and Shanghai Banking Corporation Limited (HSBC) Limited under the Stablecoin Ordinance to issue stablecoins in Hong Kong. The licenses are effective today. According to the licensees' business plans, they intend to commence operations in the coming months after the relevant preparatory work is completed.

I. Why Did Identifier Financial Technology and HSBC Become Licensed Stablecoin Issuers?

According to information on the HKMA's official website, Identifier Financial Technology Co., Ltd. and The Hongkong and Shanghai Banking Corporation Limited (HSBC) Limited have become licensed stablecoin issuers.

According to the licensees' business plans, they intend to commence operations in the coming months after the relevant preparatory work is completed.

As of the application deadline of September 30, 2025, the HKMA received applications from 36 institutions.

The Hong Kong Monetary Authority (HKMA) has always emphasized that licensing has a very high threshold, and the approval process mainly considers two aspects: (1) whether the applicant has sufficient risk management capabilities and experience, and complies with relevant regulations in Hong Kong and other regions; and (2) whether the applicant can propose specific application scenarios and feasible business solutions and development plans. Among the 36 applications, Point Point and HSBC demonstrated their ability to manage risks prudently and proposed specific application scenarios and future development plans, based on compliance with the relevant licensing requirements of the law, and were therefore granted licenses. Both licensed issuers plan to issue Hong Kong dollar stablecoins in the first phase. In summary, their business plans, application scenarios and development directions include: list-paddingleft-2">

  • Cross-border Payments: Leveraging the advantages of stablecoins and utilizing the issuer's extensive international business and partner network, while complying with the regulations and requirements set forth by regulatory authorities in other regions, to provide businesses and individuals with efficient, transparent, and lower-cost cross-border payment methods;

  • Local Payments: Utilizing existing business infrastructure and customer base, to accelerate the application of stablecoins in Hong Kong, providing individuals and merchants with a secure, fast, and efficient transaction and settlement experience;

  • Tokenized Asset Trading: As a settlement tool for tokenized asset trading, compliant stablecoins can facilitate real-time on-chain transactions, helping to explore opportunities in the tokenized asset market and improve market liquidity; at the same time, it will also explore application scenarios for using compliant stablecoins as collateral management;

  • Innovative Applications: Fully leveraging the programmable nature of stablecoins, to explore and promote innovative applications such as conditional payments and supply chain financing. Both licensed issuers have banking backgrounds and have participated in the Hong Kong Monetary Authority's experimental projects on central bank digital currencies (CBDCs) and tokenized deposits. They possess in-depth understanding of the functions and application scenarios of various digital currencies, which is conducive to exploring the possibilities of "future payments." One issuer has formed a consortium with local telecommunications, payment, and digital asset companies, enabling compliant stablecoins to leverage synergies. As their business steadily develops, both institutions plan to gradually introduce more diverse companies (such as scenario providers) to promote the application of compliant stablecoins. They will also continue to expand the application scenarios of stablecoins, explore more overseas markets, and consider introducing other currencies, depending on business operations, market conditions, and international regulatory developments.

    Risk Management Measures

    Licensed issuers are required to implement sound and comprehensive policies and risk management measures in accordance with regulatory requirements regarding reserve asset management and asset security, price stabilization mechanisms, redemption arrangements, and technological security. They must also establish robust anti-money laundering systems, including using blockchain monitoring tools in daily operations to detect suspicious transactions and take corresponding follow-up actions. They must also verify the identity of stablecoin holders themselves or through reliable third-party partners to ensure the effectiveness of monitoring measures. Both licensed issuers demonstrated in their applications their ability to comply with and implement these measures.

    Follow-up Work

    Before officially launching their compliant stablecoins, licensed issuers need to complete relevant preparatory work, including technology platform and system testing, implementation of risk management measures, and personnel resource arrangements. Based on the current business plans of the two institutions, we expect regulated stablecoins in Hong Kong to be launched successively from the middle to the second half of this year.

    After stablecoins are officially launched, the Hong Kong Monetary Authority (HKMA) will conduct continuous and effective supervision based on the business and risk nature of individual licensees, through measures such as on-site inspections, off-site inspections, independent assessments, and meetings with the management of licensees, to ensure that issuers comply with relevant regulatory requirements. We will also review the effectiveness of the implementation of the business plan submitted at the time of application and the preparation for business expansion to ensure that the policy objectives of issuing stablecoin licenses in Hong Kong are reflected. As for institutions that were not granted licenses in the first batch of applications, or other institutions that apply in the future, the HKMA will maintain communication and exchange with them, and review these applications in accordance with relevant laws and uniform regulatory standards and requirements. Regarding the issuance of additional licenses in the future and the timing, we maintain an open but cautious attitude and have no clear inclination at this stage. However, we must reiterate that considering the risks involved in the issuance business, the protection of users, and the market's capacity and long-term development, the issuance of licenses has a fairly high threshold, and even if more licenses are issued in the future, the overall number of licenses will be very limited. The operational effectiveness of the two licenses after their implementation and the market response will also help us make a more comprehensive assessment. Eddie Yue, Chief Executive of the Hong Kong Monetary Authority (HKMA), stated that the first batch of stablecoin issuers were granted licenses today. According to the licensees' business plans, they intend to commence operations in the coming months after completing relevant preparatory work. Yue expressed his expectation that issuers will commence operations as planned, actively exploring development opportunities while managing risks effectively, promoting the application of compliant stablecoins, and addressing pain points in financial and economic activities. He also urged the public to be vigilant against any fraudulent activities or scams claiming to be related to licensees or their issued stablecoins, and to use regulated channels when purchasing or using stablecoins. In case of doubt, the public should check the HKMA's records to verify the licensee's identity or contact the licensee for further enquiries. HSBC welcomed the HKMA's granting of stablecoin issuance licenses. It plans to launch a Hong Kong dollar-denominated stablecoin in the second half of 2026.

    • II. Interpretation of the Main Contents of the Hong Kong Stablecoin Ordinance

    The Hong Kong Stablecoin Ordinance was passed by the Legislative Council on May 21, 2025, and officially came into effect on August 1 of the same year. It is the world's first systematic regulatory legislation specifically targeting fiat-pegged stablecoins, and was implemented before the US GENIUS Act. The core logic of the ordinance is "license first"—anyone issuing fiat-pegged stablecoins in Hong Kong must obtain prior approval from the Monetary Authority. Unlicensed issuance is punishable by a maximum fine of HK$10 million and imprisonment for 7 years.

    1. Scope of Regulation: What are "Regulated Stablecoin Activities"? The Ordinance regulates "specified stablecoins," currently specifically referring to fiat-referenced stablecoins that appear to be entirely linked to one or more official currencies to maintain a stable value. The following three categories are regulated: issuance of specified stablecoins within Hong Kong; issuance of stablecoins outside Hong Kong but appearing to be linked to the Hong Kong dollar for value maintenance; and active promotion of such issuance activities to the Hong Kong public. It is worth noting that central bank-issued digital currencies, security tokens, and store-of-value instruments are explicitly excluded, and algorithmic stablecoins are also not within the scope of regulation. 2. Licensing Criteria: Six Core Thresholds The Ordinance requires applicants to meet a series of minimum criteria to be granted a license, and these criteria must be continuously met after obtaining the license. The main thresholds include: Firstly, incorporation status. Applicants must be local companies incorporated in Hong Kong or authorized institutions established outside Hong Kong (i.e., banks regulated by the Hong Kong Monetary Authority). Foreign non-bank institutions must first establish a subsidiary in Hong Kong, using this subsidiary as the applicant entity. This is to ensure that the Hong Kong Monetary Authority (HKMA) can effectively conduct local supervision and enforcement. Secondly, financial resources. Applicants must hold at least HK$25 million in paid-up share capital or equivalent assets, and this capital can only be used for their own business and not for transactions with related parties. Authorized institutions (banks) are exempt from this requirement. Thirdly, reserve asset management. This is the most detailed core provision in the regulations. Issuers must maintain a separate reserve asset portfolio for each specified stablecoin, and the market value must not be less than the total face value of circulating stablecoins at any time. Reserve assets must be high-quality, highly liquid, and low-risk assets, strictly segregated from the issuer's own assets through trust arrangements, and held in a licensed bank or an independent custodian institution recognized by the HKMA. Furthermore, they must be regularly audited by an independent external auditor, and the reserve composition, risk assessment, and audit results must be fully disclosed to the public. Fourthly, redemption rights. Holders have the right to redeem stablecoins at face value. Valid redemption requests must be processed within one business day of receipt, and unreasonable fees or harsh conditions may not be charged. Even if the issuer is insolvent, holders may still issue instructions regarding the disposal of reserve assets and make claims against the issuer for any shortfall. Fifth, suitability and personnel requirements. The CEO, directors, stablecoin managers (applicable to authorized institutions), and controlling shareholders must all be approved by the Hong Kong Monetary Authority (HKMA) and must meet the "suitable candidate" criteria regarding reputation, experience, and professional competence. The HKMA generally expects senior management to be based in Hong Kong. Sixth, prohibition of interest payments. Issuers may not pay any form of profit, return, or income to holders based on the holding period, face value, or market value of the stablecoins they issue. Income generated from reserve assets belongs to the issuer, not the holders—this design aims to characterize stablecoins as payment and settlement instruments, not investment products. 3. Sales Channel Restrictions: Only "Authorized Providers" Can Distribute Stablecoins. The regulations stipulate that licensees can only entrust "authorized providers" to offer stablecoins to the public. Authorized providers include: other licensed stablecoin issuers, recognized institutions (banks), licensed stored value payment instrument operators, SFC-licensed virtual asset trading platforms, and securities brokers holding Type 1 regulated activities licenses. This design integrates stablecoin distribution into the existing licensed financial institution system, fundamentally eliminating unlicensed sales. 4. Information Disclosure Obligations. Licensees must publish a white paper comprehensively detailing the basic information of the stablecoin, reserve management arrangements, issuance and redemption mechanisms, underlying technology, and usage risks; they must regularly disclose the composition of reserve assets and independent verification results; and they must establish a sound complaint handling and compensation mechanism that is independent, fair, easily accessible, and does not impose unreasonable burdens on holders. 5. Regulatory Powers and Exit Mechanisms The Hong Kong Monetary Authority (HKMA) has broad regulatory powers over licensees, including requiring information, auditing accounts, appointing advisors, and even statutory managers. Licenses can be revoked for up to 16 statutory grounds, including failure to meet minimum standards, insolvency, providing false information, and breaching license conditions. The HKMA can also temporarily suspend a license for 14 days without a hearing in emergency situations, or temporarily suspend it for up to 6 months according to standard procedures. If an issuer applies for voluntary withdrawal of its license, it must prove to the HKMA that the interests of all holders of the stablecoin have been adequately protected. This usually requires notifying holders of full redemption and submitting an auditor's report. III. Evolution of Hong Kong's Stablecoin Licensing System In October 2022, the Hong Kong SAR Government issued the "Declaration on Virtual Asset Development Policy," clarifying that the regulatory direction had shifted from "prudent observation" to "active embrace," proposing to explore stablecoin applications and establishing the principle of "same business, same risk, same regulation." This marked the formal entry of stablecoin regulation into the policy agenda. In March 2024, the Hong Kong Monetary Authority launched the "Stablecoin Issuer Sandbox Project," aiming to provide a controlled testing environment for institutions intending to issue stablecoins, allowing them to verify their business models and technical systems under regulatory exemptions. On July 18, 2024, Hong Kong announced its first three participants (in alphabetical order): Jingdong Coinlink Technology Hong Kong; RD InnoTech (a subsidiary of RD Technologies, testing the HKDR stablecoin for digital asset trading and cross-border payments); and a consortium of Standard Chartered Bank (Hong Kong) Limited, Animoca Brands Limited, and Hong Kong Telecom Limited. Participants were required to comply with sandbox requirements and were prohibited from raising funds from the public or offering investment products. It was expected that some participants would apply for formal licenses after the regulations came into effect. In December 2024, the Hong Kong government published the Stablecoin Bill. This bill marked another step forward in Hong Kong's proactive regulation of virtual assets. On May 21, 2025, the Hong Kong Legislative Council passed the Stablecoin Bill at its third reading, clarifying that the issuance of stablecoins is a licensed activity, and that a license is required for issuance pegged to the Hong Kong dollar or issued in Hong Kong. This marked the formal implementation of stablecoin regulation in Hong Kong. On May 26, 2025, the Hong Kong Monetary Authority (HKMA) released two important consultation documents: the Guidance on the Supervision of Licensed Issuers and the AML/CFT Anti-Money Laundering Guidance, aiming to safeguard the transparency, security, and sustainable development of Hong Kong's stablecoin market by establishing a standardized regulatory framework. On August 1, 2025, the Hong Kong Stablecoin Ordinance officially came into effect, explicitly stipulating that the following activities require a license: issuing stablecoins in Hong Kong, issuing Hong Kong dollar-pegged stablecoins (regardless of location), and promoting stablecoins to the Hong Kong public. This marks Hong Kong as one of the world's first jurisdictions to establish a stablecoin licensing system. The HKMA set September 30, 2025, as the deadline for submitting applications for Hong Kong stablecoin licenses. However, according to publicly available information, HKMA Deputy Chief Executive Chan Wai-man clearly stated that September 30 is not the final deadline, and institutions not included in the initial list still have a chance. On April 10, 2026, the Hong Kong Monetary Authority announced that the Commissioner of Monetary Authority had granted stablecoin issuer licenses to Identifier Financial Technology Co., Ltd. and The Hongkong and Shanghai Banking Corporation Limited under the Stablecoin Ordinance, to issue stablecoins in Hong Kong. IV. Introduction of Licensed Companies Identifier Financial Technology Co., Ltd. Identifier Financial Technology Co., Ltd. is a joint venture established by Standard Chartered Bank (Hong Kong), Hong Kong Telecom, and Animoca Brands specifically to apply for a Hong Kong stablecoin license. Standard Chartered Bank (Hong Kong) provides traditional banking compliance, risk control, and a global financial network; Hong Kong Telecom (HKT) provides a local payment network, retail channels, and telecommunications infrastructure; and Animoca Brands provides Web3, blockchain technology, and digital asset ecosystem resources. After completing system testing and risk control deployment, a compliant stablecoin is expected to be officially launched in mid-to-second-half of 2026. The Hongkong and Shanghai Banking Corporation Limited (HSBC) is a founding member of the HSBC Group and its flagship bank in the Asia-Pacific region. It is the largest registered bank in Hong Kong and one of the three note-issuing banks. HSBC has a deep presence in the Web3 space, focusing on tokenized assets, institutional custody, on-chain bonds, and deposit tokenization.

data.title: After the number of nodes dropped by 70%, Solana is now in a panic.
data.description:
Author: momo, chaincatcher

The number of Solana nodes has decreased by 70% from its historical high. In early April, according to Solana Compass data, its validator count plummeted from 2,560 in March 2023 to approximately 756; during the same period, the Satoshi Index dropped from 31 to 20, a decrease of 35%, indicating a corresponding weakening of decentralization.

data.title: My final letter to DeepSeek urging them to update!
data.description:
Hu YanpingShanghai University of Finance and EconomicsDistinguished Professor of Shanghai University of Finance and Economics, Author of the Smart Economy Series Report

Recently, the article "People Miss DeepSeek" went viral, mentioning that DeepSeek has driven down the cost of large-scale global models, allowing users and industries to enjoy cheaper tokens.

The key issue is that the rampant "token burning" of intelligent agent applications like "crayfish" has further increased user costs. In this situation, the responsibility of driving cost reduction and efficiency improvement across the entire industry has once again fallen on DeepSeek's shoulders. It's been over a year since the release of DeepSeek V3 and R1, and the outside world originally expected DeepSeek V4 to make a splash during this year's Spring Festival, but those hopes were ultimately dashed. However, judging from recent events such as system outages and the launch of expert mode, it seems that DeepSeek V4 may be getting closer to us. Therefore, this may be the last time we "urge" DeepSeek to release an update. In this letter urging me to update, I want to talk to those friends who miss DeepSeek about topics such as the narrative of AI in China, the wave of technological evolution, ecosystem competition, and token economics. The narrative of AI in China has changed. In the Spring Festival of 2025, DeepSeek R1 made its debut with low cost, high performance, and open source, reaching its peak upon release. It not only leads the field of large-scale domestic models but also sweeps the world. Internet platforms, IT giants, and various industries have all adopted it and embraced open source, with all sorts of DeepSeek all-in-one machines trying to steal the spotlight. During that period, when discussing AI in China, DeepSeek was invariably mentioned. It's no exaggeration to say that even elderly people on the street were likely talking about, or even using, this domestically developed AI assistant. However, over the past year, the AI ​​industry and China's AI narrative have become vastly different: a three-pronged narrative surrounding AI—the "Chinese team," the "Chinese chain," and the "Chinese ring"—has taken shape. DeepSeek's solo AI narrative for China has lost its luster. Therefore, from this perspective, when looking at large-scale models and AI, what everyone lacks is not just computing power and electricity, but also a window of opportunity. Regarding the "Chinese team," I summarize it as "(3+1)+6+N." The "3+1" refers to the four major companies: ByteDance, Alibaba, Tencent, and Baidu. The latter three are the three giants of the internet era, the well-known BAT (Baidu, Alibaba, Tencent). The number "6" corresponds to the "Six Little Tigers" of the big model era—Kimi, Zhipu, MiniMax, Jieyue Xingchen, Baichuan, and Mianbi Intelligence. These companies either completed their IPOs or were on their way to IPOs while DeepSeek was focused on its own research and development. The "Six Little Tigers" originally included Kai-Fu Lee's 01Wanwu, but it began to fall behind in the first "Hundred Models Battle," so we've included Mianbi Intelligence here. However, Mianbi Intelligence's presence has also gradually diminished over the past year. "N" doesn't refer to a single company; it represents other vertical model and professional market AI companies. A total of 10 companies/categories constitute the leading force in China's large model industry. They are no longer isolated players, but a competitive industrial group, and a formidable opponent that DeepSeek must overcome on its path to renewed success. Growing alongside the "Chinese team" is also the "Chinese blockchain"—from chip computing power, clusters/cloud, data corpora, algorithms/models, intelligent agents, and AI application development ecosystem, it has completed the entire chain connection, becoming one of only two countries globally with a complete intelligent technology industry chain. It has the potential to provide more options for global intelligent infrastructure and to offer new public goods for global intelligent inclusion through the export of capabilities in the capabilities economy. This shouldn't be doubted; while DeepSeek R1 certainly established the brand of Chinese models overseas, manufacturers like MiniMax are now also doing very well in overseas markets. Regarding the "China Ring," it covers three aspects: industry, application, and investment—an industrial closed loop from AI to AI4S and then to modern industrial clusters; a market application closed loop from AI technology to thousands of industries and millions of households; and a capital closed loop from early-stage investment to IPO exit. The initial formation of this closed loop not only signifies the successful implementation of artificial intelligence in China but also indicates the interconnection of large and small cycles at different levels of the intelligent economy. From groups and chains to rings, China's AI narrative has already changed. Excluding free marketing strategies by companies, since the beginning of 2026, the Six Little Tigers model has consistently led in token consumption on international platforms such as OpenRouter, with an overall share exceeding half, primarily driven by overseas users. In summary, by 2025, China's open-source capabilities will have reshaped the global landscape of artificial intelligence development. By 2026, China's AI development will have entered a phase of exporting its capabilities. From a global perspective and within the AI ​​industry, the diversification of technological pathways enhances talent mobility and improves supply chain resilience. For downstream application developers, the availability of multiple suppliers translates to stronger bargaining power and lower lock-in risk. Another positive aspect of China's AI narrative is that the market is not yet monopolized by a few oligopolies. This is beneficial for competitive innovation and talent ecosystem development, and also helps to create a cluster advantage in the Sino-US AI competition. The Four Waves Have Passed Chinese classical mythology often says, "One day in heaven is one year on earth." In the year that DeepSeek has been "inactive," four waves of artificial intelligence have passed—programming, multimodal, intelligent agents, and OpenClaw (crayfish). While AI programming tools like GitHub Copilot, Cursor, and Claude Code have swept the developer community, DeepSeek's existence is easily forgotten in the story of Vibe Coding, even though it is also used in programming scenarios. Programming, the underlying driver of artificial intelligence sweeping across all industries and the most essential scenario for developers, is now firmly dominated by companies like Anthropic abroad, while in China it has become a battlefield for companies like Kimi to expand their territory. In this wave of multimodal computing, Gemini 3 Pro and others have shown impressive performance in visual understanding and image generation. Nano Banana is a name that comes to mind, while in video generation, it's ByteDance's Seedance 2.0. DeepSeek, on the other hand, seems to be a slow starter, only beginning its gray-scale testing of a million token contexts with V3.2, and its multimodal capabilities are still far from being realized. Some say that in the field of large-scale models, if a product's technological path is wrong, it will miss an entire era. Is DeepSeek stuck here? It's hard to say. The third wave is Agent-Multi-Agent-Swarm Intelligence. Compared to the understanding and conversational abilities of AI assistants, agents have evolved to the execution level, shifting from "answering questions" to "solving questions"—in the past it was "passive response," now it's "active execution." In this wave, the emergence of products like Manus signifies that AI Agents are moving from concept to reality, while Kimi Agent Swarm has pushed this wave to its climax. In this wave, DeepSeek was primarily used as a model rather than a builder of the agent ecosystem, and the model itself had limited support for agents, tools, and code. By 2026, a wave of action intelligence, represented by OpenClaw and various Claws, Claude Code, and Claude Cowork, began to emerge. Their capabilities had actually transcended the agent level, becoming a takeover-type application operating system—an application AI OS. However, products like OpenClaw were jokingly referred to as "token black holes," as their token consumption per task was tens or even hundreds of times that of traditional conversational AI. This high-input, low-output model faces sustainability challenges in large-scale industrial applications. The product's roughness, instability, and multiple disruptive iterations are akin to a poorly constructed house. Therefore, it's not surprising that some are saying "people miss DeepSeek," given that it has disappeared after several waves of development, and that people need it to drive cost reduction and efficiency improvement in China's large-scale models. However, it must be said that OpenClaw confirms the validity of the logic behind applying AI OS and general-purpose intelligent agents, and that the time is right. It tells everyone that AI is no longer just a tool, but can be an all-powerful, takeover-type agent. So, during the March "shrimp farming craze," you can see how quickly everyone copied each other's methods. To promote local products, everyone started giving away "cyber eggs" (internet-enabled eggs). This is because OpenClaw made major companies, including Anthropic, instantly realize that an all-encompassing application OS and mobile intelligence were within reach. They had brains, could execute tasks, and growing arms and legs to become general-purpose intelligent agents was easy! This is also why Anthropic reacted and countered the fastest, and had the biggest impact on Claw. Claude Code attacked OpenClaw's escape route, and other major companies quickly copied Claude Code and OpenClaw's methods. This is what is happening right now. The reason this is so crucial is that its entry point, immense value, and future ecological dominance are no less than that of models or the previous three waves of innovation. If large models are accumulating power, multimodal approaches are expanding scenarios, and agents represent sowing seeds, then large-scale harvesting of the ecosystem relies on the application of AI OS and general-purpose intelligent agents, which already carries some hints of an endgame and ultimate form. However, the situation may change when we reach the EI (intrinsic intelligence) and II (autonomous intelligence) stages. However, considering OpenClaw's return on investment today, it's unlikely to be the one that will occupy the ecological niche of AI OS and general-purpose intelligent agents. So, in this final letter urging DeepSeek to release more content, we also want to raise a question: Is DeepSeek, by not immediately jumping into these four areas, choosing to conserve its strength, hoping to "make a big splash" with V4 and subsequent foundational models? However, the market never waits. User attention, developer enthusiasm, and capital flows are all being diverted in wave after wave. The competitive barriers in the fields where these four waves occur have risen sharply, and the costs of ecosystem development have also increased significantly. Is DeepSeek's story destined to end with the Spring Festival of 2025? The Arrival of Full Ecosystem Competition

The Arrival of Full Ecosystem Competition

Previously, my view was that leading companies had entered the stage of full ecosystem competition. In this stage, full-stack AI capabilities are the foundation for the next round of competition among giants; the best example is Google.

The Arrival of Full Ecosystem Competition

My previous view was that leading companies had already entered the stage of full ecosystem competition. In this stage, full-stack AI capabilities are the foundation for the next round of competition among giants; Google is the best example.

The Arrival of Full Ecosystem Competition

Previously, my view was that leading companies had entered the stage of full ecosystem competition.

Expectations for V4 are not the standards of the past

The suspense across the ocean is: to what extent can OpenAI and Anthropic's next-generation models compete? Can Super Apps become application operating systems and general-purpose intelligent agents like the increasingly ecosystem-building Claude Code? And which will be the fastest in coding, the underlying sharp knife of the ecosystem? These three things will influence the major trends this year.

Judging from the current situation, Anthropic's success is almost reaching OpenAI's stronghold. This can be seen from the financial data of the two companies disclosed by the Wall Street Journal. Anthropic may be able to turn a profit before OpenAI.

data.title: Covenant AI's exit caused TAO's stock to plummet 17%, signaling a crisis for Bittensor.
data.description:
Tao Zhu, Jinse Finance

Summary

On April 9, 2026, Covenant AI, the main subnet developer on Bittensor, announced its withdrawal from the Bittensor network ecosystem. Covenant claims that Bittensor co-founder Jacob Steeves has control over the network and accuses Bittensor's promise of decentralization of being "a lie."

Why is Covenant AI leaving Bittensor? How did Bittensor respond? Why did Covenant AI's withdrawal cause Bittensor to plummet by 17%?

I. Covenant AI's Statement

Covenant AI founder Sam Dare issued a statement on X, pointing out that the reason for Covenant AI's departure from Bittensor is the centralized power of Bittensor co-founder Jacob Steeves. The three-way alliance structure (multi-signature for network upgrades by three people, claiming to the community that this is distributed governance) is just a show of decentralization.

Why is Covenant AI leaving Bittensor?

Why is Covenant AI leaving Bittensor?

I. Covenant AI's Statement

Covenant AI founder Sam Dare issued a statement on X, pointing out that the reason for Covenant AI's departure from Bittensor is the centralized power of Bittensor co-founder Jacob Steeves. The three-way alliance structure (multi-signature for network upgrades by three people, claiming to the community that this is distributed governance) is just a show of decentralization.

On April 9, 2026, Covenant AI announced its withdrawal from Bittensor due to the centralized power of Bittensor co-founder Jacob Steeves.

... Jacob Steeves effectively controls the three-person governing team, capable of unilaterally deploying changes at any time without any procedures or consensus, with the other members acting as legal shields. The specific statement is as follows: Covenant AI is built on a simple belief: the training of cutting-edge AI models should not be controlled by any single entity. Over the past two years, our team has delivered on this vision. Covenant-72B—a model with 72 billion parameters, collaboratively trained by over 70 independent contributors using general-purpose hardware in a permissionless environment—is arguably the largest decentralized large-scale language model (LLM) pre-training project in history. It not only garnered recognition from Nvidia's CEO and was cited by Anthropic's co-founder, but it also propelled the ecosystem we built to achieve a value surge of up to 90%. We never sought attention intentionally. We simply wanted to prove that decentralized training models work. The attention that followed was merely a testament to the power of our work. We hope to provide an unambiguous and clear explanation of what all this means. When a single entity can arbitrarily halt the token distribution of a subnet; can override the owner and forcibly take over its management of its own community space; can blatantly abandon a specific project without any established procedures; and can even distort token sales into a coercive mechanism to force others to comply—this is not true "decentralization." It is merely "centralized control" disguised as "decentralization." Every participant, miner, validator, and investor in this ecosystem must be keenly aware that: This totalitarian power truly exists; it has been exercised by Jacob Steeves (i.e., Const); and its purpose is not to maintain the healthy development of the network, but to re-establish control over a team—a team that has become increasingly independent and difficult to manage. For those whose power is entirely based on the fact that "everyone depends on him," any subnet owner capable of independently building communities, making independent decisions, and operating freely without permission is undoubtedly a potential threat. The problems of decentralization are far more complex than any single event can suggest. Bittensor employs a "three-way alliance" operational architecture: three specific individuals jointly manage multisignature (multisig) permissions related to network upgrades; however, when promoting this architecture to the community, it is embellished as a kind of "distributed governance" model. This is far from the truth. It's nothing more than a clumsy "decentralization" political show. Jacob Steeves actually maintains firm control over the real power of this "three-way alliance," vehemently resisting any substantial decentralization and unilaterally pushing for changes anytime, anywhere—without any established procedures or consensus. As for the nominal members within it, their only value is to act as legal "shields"—in the event of accountability or litigation, they will be the first to bear legal responsibility, while Jacob himself will remain unscathed. This online community talks a lot about so-called "governance" and "decentralization," but has never put them into practice. The reins of power have never left the hands of any one person. This is the crux of the problem. Bittensor's entire core premise—the promise that attracted developers, miners, validators, and investors to this ecosystem—is that no single entity can control it. However, this promise is actually a lie. We cannot continue building on a network where our fundamental promise to investors—that the infrastructure is decentralized and permissionless—is at odds with the reality of its actual governance. We cannot raise funds, recruit talent, or ask the community to contribute resources on a foundation that can be arbitrarily destroyed by anyone. We absolutely do not want to pass this risk on to those who trust us. Therefore, with deep regret and frustration, we hereby announce that Covenant AI is officially withdrawing from the Bittensor network. Over the past few weeks, Jacob Steeves (also known as "Const") has taken a series of actions against the operations of Covenant AI that fundamentally contradict the principles the network claims to uphold. These actions include: suspending the distribution of token rewards (Emissions) to our subnets; stripping us of administrative privileges within our own community channels; unilaterally abandoning our subnet infrastructure; and exerting direct economic pressure at a critical moment in our operational conflict through a large-scale, high-profile token sell-off. These are by no means governance decisions made through transparent, decentralized consensus mechanisms. They are merely punitive retaliations by one person—who has never truly relinquished control of the network, despite having publicly declared to the world that he was no longer responsible for its operation. Covenant AI's mission has never changed. Decentralized, permissionless AI training is not a feature unique to Bittensor; it's a technological capability our team is passionately working to advance. Our research, our team members, our model assets, and our grand vision will all go with us. We are currently working on a series of exciting projects and initiatives, and related news and announcements will be officially released to the public soon. To our community members, mining partners, and every supporter who has contributed computing power, time, and faith to the Covenant-72B project: you have proven that things once deemed "impossible" are indeed entirely achievable. The proof of this great achievement does not reside on a single Discord server, nor is it dependent on the governance architecture of any particular network; it is deeply rooted in our research, our AI models, and the very soul of our team. Wherever we choose our next base of operations, we will continue to work diligently to earn your trust and support. Decentralization is not merely a marketing slogan—it will not be allowed to be arbitrarily overturned or trampled upon when this concept becomes "out of place" or "obstructive" for Const. It is a solemn commitment to every builder, every miner, and every investor—they entered this ecosystem based on the promise that "no individual could possibly do what we are doing now." Either make this system truly decentralized, or stop pretending it is. Sam Dare, Founder of Covenant AI (blockquote) II. Response from Bittensor Co-founder Jacob Steeves (blockquote) Jacob Steeves did not initially respond directly, only saying: This will mark the birth of the first subnets on Bittensor, which will operate in a "headless" mode and exist as a true commodity. Following this, Steeves continued: Exploitations can teach a system about its weaknesses. The faster you discover them, the faster you learn. Tonight's achievement is that Bittensor will invent a locking-based subnet ownership mechanism—specifically, subnet ownership depends on the team's long-term financial investment in the project. This means: 1) Investors can see well in advance whether the owners have unlocked their tokens; 2) They can reprice subnets before the owners; 3) They can flexibly transfer their funds to another team or agency to manage the system. Thanks to @DistStateAndMe for helping to further advance Bittensor's decentralization and developing a solution to one of the oldest problems in the cryptocurrency space: founders deceiving token holders. Looking forward to training some 1T parameter models with expert miners in the field. "The dead never rise again." III. Impact on Bittensor Before its withdrawal, Covenant AI was widely regarded as one of the key growth engines of the Bittensor ecosystem. The Covenant-72B model achieved a major breakthrough: a large language model with 72 billion parameters; collaboratively trained by 70+ independent contributors; completed on general-purpose hardware in a permissionless environment. It was hailed as one of the largest decentralized LLM pre-training experiments in history. Specifically, training a 72 billion parameter model typically requires a tightly coupled cluster of high-end GPUs connected by InfiniBand—hardware that costs tens of millions of dollars and requires latency between nodes to be below milliseconds. Traditionally, it was believed that ordinary internet connections could never bridge this gap. Covenant-72B directly challenges this view, employing SparseLoCo technology—which combines sparsity, 2-bit quantization, and error feedback—resulting in over 146 times less inter-node communication compared to previous distributed operations. The result is that Bittensor's Templar subnet, trained on over 70 peer nodes using 1.1 trillion tokens, and validated using the Gauntlet system, which rewards high-quality nodes (TAO) and penalizes poorly performing nodes using loss assessment and OpenSkill ranking. The endorsement from prominent figures like Jensen Huang and Chamas Palihapitia boosted Bittensor's credibility and strengthened investor confidence in the ecosystem. Therefore, Covenant AI is one of the core narrative drivers in the TAO surge. However, as the event unfolded, the most significant impact on Bittensor was reflected in the market. Following the announcement, the TAO price plummeted and has not yet shown a significant rebound as of this writing. It closed at $264.09, down 17.7%.

Furthermore, as mentioned in Covenant AI, Bittensor suffers from a false sense of decentralization. Therefore, it remains to be seen whether developers and investors will lose confidence in Bittensor. For a long time, Bittensor has been tied to the core narrative of decentralized AI training, and Covenant AI's exit directly impacts the future prospects of this narrative.

Some observers on X pointed out:

  • Will the future of TAO tokens gradually lead to zero due to internal power struggles and divisions within the project team?

    In addition, as mentioned in Covenant AI, Bittensor suffers from a false sense of decentralization.

    Will the future of TAO tokens gradually lead to zero?

    How would you handle the power distribution between subnet owners and the entire ecosystem? I don't care about decentralization: I only care about price.

  • So Sam Dare is the Sam Bankman-Fried of the Bittensor ecosystem.

  • A silly question. Given this governance issue, isn't now the best time to focus on subnets governed by agents rather than humans?

  • The short-term pain of $TAO will eventually bring long-term benefits.

  • We need to fundamentally increase the cost of malicious exits, reduce discontent through legitimate governance, and build a strong enough sense of platform identity so that leaving the platform is a real loss.

Conclusion

In this debate, the concept of decentralization contradicts actual power: Covenant AI expressed its disagreement with centralized power and chose to eventually exit; while Jacob Steeves emphasized mechanism evolution and downplayed the power issue.

In the short term, the market has already reflected this uncertainty in its prices; however, in the long term, the key variable determining TAO's value lies in whether Bittensor can restore governance trust, attract new high-quality subnets, and maintain its decentralized AI narrative.

data.title: Coinbase Partners MarketVector To Launch New Index Combining BTC And Tokenised Gold For Institutional Investors
data.description:
Bitcoin And Tokenised Gold Meet In New Store-Of-Value Benchmark

MarketVector Indexes and Coinbase Asset Management have launched a new benchmark that combines Bitcoin with tokenised gold, aiming to track how investors are thinking about capital preservation in a period of heavy debt and persistent inflation pressure.

The new Coinbase Store of Value Index is built around BTC and Pax Gold (PAXG), one of the largest gold-backed tokens.

It is designed as a price-return index in US dollars and uses a rules-based approach rather than a fixed split between the two assets.

Coinbase and MarketVector launched the Coinbase Store of Value Index, tracking Bitcoin and tokenized gold. It uses a dynamic, rules-based allocation method, adjusting weights based on relative volatility to balance Bitcoin's growth potential with gold's historical stability. pic.twitter.com/YXRkT94bU1

— Bitcoin Bandit (@Birchley23) April 10, 2026

Why The Index Looks Beyond Gold Alone

Gold has long been seen as a safe place for wealth, but Bitcoin is increasingly being treated in a similar way by some investors.

The idea behind the index is to reflect that shift, especially as demand grows for scarce assets that are not tied to government debt or money printing.

Anthony Bassili, President at Coinbase Asset Management, said:

“We believe this index represents a meaningful evolution in store-of-value investing. In a world of fiscal dominance, the future belongs to scarce assets that cannot be printed and are not an obligation of a government or private issuer.”

Gold has long defined “store of value," but that definition is evolving. We’ve partnered with @CoinbaseAM to launch $COINSOV, a rules-based benchmark combining Bitcoin + gold in a volatility-aware framework. Built to help investors navigate today’s macro environment.
Learn more:… pic.twitter.com/AoggNRjwDm

— MarketVector Indexes (@MarketVector) April 9, 2026

How The Allocation Changes

The index does not hold Bitcoin and gold at fixed weights.

Instead, it uses inverse volatility to guide allocation, giving more weight to the asset that has been less volatile at the time.

It is rebalanced every quarter, allowing the mix to adjust as market conditions change.

MarketVector said its research, covering 2017 through 2025, suggests the model delivered better risk-adjusted returns than simpler approaches.

It also showed smaller drawdowns than a basic 50/50 allocation, while outperforming several widely followed benchmarks over the same period.

Why Institutions May Pay Attention

The launch comes as Bitcoin’s store-of-value case faces more scrutiny.

Over the past year, it has often traded more like a risk asset, moving alongside equities at times.

That has prompted some investors to question whether it still behaves like digital gold in all market conditions.

Martin Leinweber, Director of Digital Asset Research and Strategy at MarketVector, said the index reflects rising demand for hybrid investment solutions as macro uncertainty deepens.

Major signal from the indexing world.

MarketVector, the same company that runs the MarketVector Digital Assets 100 Index, just launched the Coinbase Store of Value Index.

It tracks Bitcoin and tokenized gold together.

Not Bitcoin and tech stocks. Not Bitcoin and crypto… pic.twitter.com/DSIGCmSq5P

— Taurus (@Taurus4BTC) April 10, 2026

MarketVector, which is based in Europe and acts as a regulated benchmark administrator, has already expanded beyond traditional indexing into digital assets with products such as the MarketVector Digital Assets 100 Index and the Coinbase 50 Index.

The new benchmark is meant to sit at the crossroads of old and new stores of value, giving institutions a structured way to combine Bitcoin’s upside with gold’s steadier profile.
